Theory: The Systemic Tau Paradigm¶

The Systemic Tau framework proposes a fundamental shift in how we understand and measure time within complex systems. Instead of treating time as a homogeneous, external background (Chronos), it introduces the Discrete Extramental Clock (RECD), an internal metric of time generated by the system’s own structural reorganization (Kairos).

Three-Layer Ontology¶

The framework operationalizes this concept through a strict mathematical and ontological three-layer architecture:

  1. Layer 1 (Local Intensification): Measures local persistence through hyper-persistence of ordinal states and structural trapping using Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA).

  2. Layer 2 (Relational Coherence): Identifies Joint Episodes (relational kairoi). This layer detects enabling windows where modules within the system become anti-synchronized, creating the necessary conditions for a global shift.

  3. Layer 3 (Ontological Ascent): Detects the exact moment of global structural reorganization. By analyzing the distance between probability distributions (e.g., Kolmogorov-Smirnov contrasts) and network topology (Frobenius norm shifts), this layer flags the emergence of a new law of temporal discreteness.

Fractal Dimension¶

The paradigm establishes that the accumulated discrete time $T$ converges to a fractal dimension $D approx 1.98$, reflecting the geometric properties of the underlying chaotic dynamics defined by the Feigenbaum constant.

systemictau
